items.q: What is the Walker circulation and how does ENSO disrupt it?
items.correct: Walker circulation is the east-west tropical Pacific atmospheric loop: trade winds push warm water west, air rises in Indonesia, sinks over Peru. El Nino weakens it, La Nina strengthens it.
items.misconception: El Nino is a current that flows from Peru to Australia.
items.random_wrong: Walker circulation is the rotation of the Earth around the sun.
items.why: Students treat El Nino as a place or current; it's an oscillating state of a coupled ocean-atmosphere system. Walker circulation is the normal state — ENSO refers to its periodic flipping between phases.

items.q: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative data?
items.correct: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable (traffic counts, river width); qualitative data is descriptive and non-numerical (interview quotes, photo descriptions, field sketches).
items.misconception: Quantitative data is always more scientific and reliable than qualitative.
items.random_wrong: Qualitative data means high-quality data and quantitative means a lot of data.
items.why: Both have value: numbers show patterns; words explain why. Mixed-methods studies combine them. Students wrongly assume numbers are objective and words are subjective — both can be done well or poorly.

items.correct: Snow accumulates in a north-east-facing hollow, compacts to ice, and rotational movement combined with plucking and abrasion deepens the hollow into an armchair shape; meltwater can fill it as a tarn.
items.misconception: A corrie is just a small lake at the top of a mountain.
items.random_wrong: A corrie forms when a river dries up high in the mountains.
items.why: The corrie is the eroded hollow; the tarn (lake) is just the post-glacial water that fills it. Aspect matters — north-east in the Northern Hemisphere because of shade and prevailing wind.

items.q: Which African region suffers severe desertification?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sahel. “Maghreb” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the maximum rainfall in hot deserts?
items.why: Correct answer: 250mm. “100mm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What percentage of Northern Hemisphere is permafrost?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
